title: Adaptive Surrogate Gradients for Sequential Reinforcement Learning in Spiking Neural Networks

reviews.summary: This paper studies how surrogate gradient slope choices affect optimization dynamics in deep spiking neural networks (SNNs) and leverages these insights to develop a sequential, off-policy reinforcement learning algorithm for continuous control. The authors empirically characterize a slope–magnitude–alignment trade-off and introduce fixed/interval/adaptive schedules, then propose TD3BC+JSRL: a spiking actor with an asymmetric (ANN) critic and a privileged “jump-start” actor to bridge the warm-up period needed by SNNs. On a challenging Crazyflie quadrotor task with curriculum learning and sim-to-real deployment, the method achieves substantially higher rewards than BC, TD3BC, and vanilla TD3, while demonstrating the feasibility of sequence-based SNN training without frame stacking.
strengths: An informative empirical analysis of surrogate gradient slopes in deep SNNs that disentangles gradient magnitude propagation from directional (cosine) alignment, highlighting a practical trade-off that is especially relevant to RL.
Introduction of adaptive surrogate slope scheduling driven by reward level and trend, which operationalizes an exploration-to-exploitation annealing mechanism suitable for SNNs.

A practical algorithmic contribution (TD3BC+JSRL) that combines a privileged guide actor and an off-policy spiking actor with BC regularization, explicitly designed to bridge SNN warm-up and enable sequence-based training.

Asymmetric actor-critic design (spiking actor, ANN critic with privileged action history) that pragmatically exploits ANN stability without sacrificing the deployability and temporal processing of SNNs.
Real-world deployment on a Crazyflie quadrotor, beyond simulation, with qualitative demonstrations of flight maneuvers and quantitative performance/error reporting.
Ablation isolating the contribution of the BC term and jump-start component; experiments with multiple seeds; and evaluation across both offline and online training regimes.
Hardware/compute profiling via NeuroBench highlighting activation sparsity and operation types (AC vs MAC), relevant to neuromorphic efficiency.
Clear problem statement around SNN warm-up and short-horizon data collection in early RL training; good motivation for jump-starting and for slope scheduling.
Algorithmic objective is stated concretely with warm-up masking, sequence handling, and BC term annealing.
Figures and narratives effectively communicate the slope trade-offs and training dynamics under curriculum learning.
Addresses an important and underexplored obstacle for SNNs in continuous control: enabling genuine sequence training without frame stacking when early policies crash.
Offers practical guidance on surrogate gradient configuration and scheduling that could benefit a broad range of spiking RL tasks.
Demonstrates viable sim-to-real performance for an energy-conscious controller that uses temporal dynamics instead of explicit history.
weaknesses: The cosine-similarity “alignment” analysis treats a steep slope (k=100) as a proxy for a “truer” gradient, which is heuristic given the non-differentiability of the Heaviside and lacks a theoretical justification; conclusions about “directional noise” could be more rigorously supported.

The adaptive slope rule directly maps reward and its derivative to k via a simple average/clamp; although effective, the choice is ad hoc and its robustness across tasks/environments is not established.

The actor is SNN-only while the critic and guide receive privileged action history; the reliance on privileged inputs and a pre-trained guide (that hovers for warm-up) is a strong practical assumption that may not generalize to other setups.
The main empirical validation is on a single (albeit challenging) quadrotor task; broader benchmarking (even in simulation) on other continuous control domains would strengthen generality claims.

Comparisons omit several strong or natural baselines: (i) on-policy SNN-PPO or recurrent PPO as an end-to-end sequence baseline, (ii) TD3/SAC with recurrent or frame-stacked ANN actors (and/or with parameter-space noise) to isolate the effect of slope-induced noise vs standard exploration, (iii) “kickstarting”/DAgger-style or online demonstration-driven methods (e.g., Kickstarting RL, DQfD/AWAC-style online finetuning) adapted to SNNs.

No sensitivity analysis for the adaptive slope scheduler (e.g., choice of averaging window, reward scaling, bounds 1–100) or for warm-up length and sequence length; it is unclear how brittle the approach is to these choices.

The SNN vs ANN comparisons use different architectures and history settings, which hinders fairness; the SNN is larger, and the ANN “no-history” baseline predictably fails on a partially observable task.

Notation overloading: “s” is used for both spikes and states in different sections, which can confuse readers; formalizing the data distribution/inputs used for the gradient analyses would help reproducibility and interpretation.
The jump-start schedule (how N increases, exact policy-switching schedule) is described informally; concrete schedules or parameters belong in the main text or a clearly referenced appendix.
Minor mismatches in claims reporting (e.g., early abstract mentions ×2.1 improvements, while later text emphasizes ×4.5 epoch reductions) could be harmonized.

Limited discussion and empirical comparison to established demonstration-aided online RL (Kickstarting RL) and online/offline RL with behavior regularization (e.g., AWAC, IQL variants, SAC+BC), which seem closely related to TD3BC+JSRL.
Recurrent/value-based sequence learners with burn-in (e.g., R2D2/IMPALA-style) are discussed but not empirically instantiated as baselines.
Parameter-space noise and entropy-based exploration are cited but not compared to the proposed slope-annealing in controlled ablations.
reviews.questions:
1. In the cosine-similarity analysis, how were inputs and weights sampled, and over what distribution were gradients averaged? Can you justify using k=100 as a “more truthful” gradient proxy beyond qualitative intuition?
2. How sensitive is the adaptive slope scheduler to reward scaling, the averaging horizon (10), and the clipping range [1, 100]? Did you try alternative reward-normalization schemes?
3. Can you report results for TD3/SAC with parameter-space noise or higher action noise, to disentangle benefits of slope-induced gradient noise from more conventional exploration?
4. How does performance vary with warm-up length and sequence length (n_warm-up, n_seq)? Is there a regime where the warm-up is short enough that TD3 without jump-start can succeed?
5. Could you include a baseline with a recurrent (or frame-stacked) ANN actor trained with TD3/SAC and a DAgger/Kickstarting-style demonstration schedule, to compare directly against JSRL+BC in the non-spiking setting?
6. What is the computational/training cost to obtain the privileged guide, and how sensitive is the final SNN performance to the guide’s quality (e.g., ablate weaker guides and report outcomes)?
7. For the offline baselines (BC, TD3BC), can you report results under a fixed (non-evolving) reward to separate “offline can’t adapt to curriculum” from “offline training per se,” and/or consider online fine-tuning of TD3BC?
8. In sim-to-real, what measures ensured safety given oscillatory behavior? Did you evaluate robustness to disturbances or sensor noise?
9. Energy estimates: what time base do the 9.7e-5 mJ numbers correspond to (per timestep, per episode, per inference), and on which hardware assumptions? Any preliminary measurements on a neuromorphic chip?
10. Can you provide per-layer gradient statistics during actual RL training (not just a synthetic setup) to corroborate the vanishing vs. noise trade-off under different slope schedules?
reviews.assessment: This paper tackles a timely and practically important problem—how to effectively train sequence-based spiking controllers for continuous control when early rollouts are short and unstable—through a well-motivated combination of empirical insight (surrogate gradient slope scheduling) and algorithmic design (TD3BC+JSRL). The sim-to-real Crazyflie results and ablations are compelling, and the slope scheduling findings are likely to be useful to practitioners. The main reservations concern the limited scope of evaluation (largely one domain), heuristic nature of the “alignment” analysis, and missing comparisons to several strong non-spiking and demonstration-driven baselines. Despite these gaps, the work presents a meaningful advance for neuromorphic RL and real-world SNN control. I lean toward acceptance, provided the authors address baseline coverage and clarify the gradient analysis and scheduler sensitivity.
